data.title: Wall Street Snaps Back On Greenland Deal, Bank Earnings Boost
data.description:
Wall Street closed higher on Wednesday, snapping a sharp selloff from the previous session, after news that the US had reached a framework agreement related to Greenland, easing concerns over fresh tariffs on European allies.

The Dow Jones Industrial Average rose 588.64 points or 1.21% to end at 49,077.23, while the S&P 500 gained 78.76 points or 1.16% to 6,875.62, marking its biggest one-day percentage gain in two months.

The Nasdaq Composite advanced 270.50 points or 1.18% to 23,224.83.

Markets were buoyed after US President Donald Trump said a framework for a future deal on Greenland and the broader Arctic region had been reached, adding that previously planned tariffs on European countries scheduled for Feb 1 would not be imposed.

The rebound followed Tuesday’s broad selloff, the worst daily performance for all three benchmarks since October, as investors reacted positively to the avoidance of a potential escalation in trade tensions.

Analysts said the announcement allowed markets to refocus on economic fundamentals, particularly corporate earnings.

Banking stocks were among the strongest performers, with the regional banking index jumping 4.7% to its highest close since November 2024, supported by upbeat earnings from several lenders.

Citizens Financial Group surged 7.1% to a record high after posting a 31.7% jump in quarterly profit, while Truist Financial rose 1.8% on higher interest income and investment banking fees.

All S&P 500 sectors ended the session in positive territory, led by energy stocks. Halliburton climbed 4.1% after beating earnings estimates, while EQT Corp and Expand Energy advanced 6.5% and 4.5% respectively as natural gas prices rose on cold weather.

Airline stocks also gained after United Airlines rose 2.2% on a stronger-than-expected outlook for the first quarter and the full year. Delta Air Lines, American Airlines and Southwest Airlines added between 1.1% and 2.4%.

Netflix, however, fell 2.2% after issuing a muted outlook and announcing a pause in share buybacks to fund the acquisition of Warner Bros Discovery’s studio and streaming businesses.

Market participants said the rally reflected relief that tariff threats had been rolled back, reducing uncertainty at a time when investors remain sensitive to geopolitical risks and policy shifts.

Despite the strong rebound, analysts cautioned that volatility could persist as markets continue to track developments in global trade relations, earnings momentum and policy signals from Washington.

Reuters

data.title: Ringgit Opens Higher Ahead Of BNM Policy Decision
data.description:
The ringgit opened higher against the US dollar on Thursday, as investors priced in expectations that Bank Negara Malaysia (BNM) would keep the overnight policy rate (OPR) unchanged at 2.75% during its policy meeting later in the day.

At 8 am, the local currency strengthened to 4.0350/0570 versus the greenback, up from Wednesday’s close of 4.0445/0495.

The firmer tone in the US dollar was largely offset by market anticipation of BNM’s decision, which is now drawing the focus of traders and investors, as reported by Bernama.

The ringgit also gained against a basket of major currencies in early trade. It rose against the Japanese yen to 2.5462/5603 from 2.5598/5631, firmed against the British pound to 5.4142/4437 from 5.4237/4304, and strengthened versus the euro to 4.7101/7357 from 4.7337/7395.

Against regional peers, the local currency was broadly higher. It appreciated versus the Singapore dollar to 3.1389/1565 from 3.1502/1543, against the Indonesian rupiah to 238.1/239.7 from 238.7/239.3, versus the Thai baht to 12.8831/9612 from 13.0086/0301, and against the Philippine peso to 6.80/6.85 from 6.82/6.83.

Market attention remains firmly on BNM’s Monetary Policy Committee decision later today.

data.title: FBM KLCI Edges Higher Amid Mixed Market Sentiment
data.description:
The FBM KLCI eked out modest gains by midday, rising 2.97 points or 0.17% to 1,702.03 at 12.30 pm, as investors remained cautious ahead of corporate earnings and regional cues.

The broader market was mixed, with the FBM70 declining 18.85 points or 0.11% to 17,378.98, while the FBMEMAS added 13.19 points to 12,528.39 and the FBMSHA gained 10.45 points to 12,203.16.

Trading activity was dominated by small-cap stocks, with Velesto leading the volume charts at 789,080 shares, closing 1 sen higher at RM0.29.

Other active counters included SBS at RM0.24 with 511,567 shares traded, and Zetrix at RM0.82, up 1.5 sen on 411,917 shares.

Among the top gainers, DLADY led the charge, adding 44 sen to RM31.72, followed by F&N and MPI, which each rose 44 sen to RM36.16 and RM34.00 respectively.

HLFG climbed 34 sen to RM20.62, while Petronas Gas gained 32 sen to RM18.74.

On the flip side, UTDPLT was the heaviest decliner, falling 56 sen to RM30.50, with Nestle down 50 sen at RM115.50.

BKawanan and KLK each lost 18 sen, closing at RM20.12 and RM20.02 respectively, while Malayan Cement shed 16 sen to RM7.40.

Market observers noted the cautious sentiment reflected a mix of profit-taking in selected counters and steady buying in defensive and blue-chip stocks.

The trading is expected to remain selective in the afternoon session as investors monitor ongoing corporate developments and global market cues.

data.title: HSIF Rebounds From Intraday Low, Uptrend Remains Intact
data.description:
RHB Investment Bank Bhd (RHB Research) maintained its long position call on Hang Seng Index Futures (HSIF), citing easing bearish momentum and a resilient bullish structure.

The research house said HSIF rose 108 points on Wednesday to close at 26,570 points after opening at 26,460. The index initially slipped to an intraday low of 26,301 before rebounding to a high of 26,719, forming a bullish candlestick. During the evening session, HSIF added a further 46 points to last trade at 26,616.

RHB Research noted that selling pressure has moderated, with support emerging at the 20-day simple moving average. The relative strength index has also started to trend higher, signalling an improvement in bullish momentum. The index remains above both its 20- and 50-day SMA lines, indicating that the broader uptrend remains intact.

“As long as the index stays above the 25,000-point support, the current retracement should be viewed as a pullback within an ongoing uptrend,” the research house said, adding that it continues to maintain a positive trading bias.

RHB Research reiterated its recommendation for traders to stay on the long position initiated at 21,416 points, with a stop-loss set at 25,000 points to manage downside risks.

The initial support level is pegged at 25,600 points, followed by 25,000 points, while immediate resistance is seen at 27,400 points, with a further upside target at 28,000 points.

data.title: European Stocks Fall, Dollar Drops, Markets Focus On Trump’s Tariff Threat
data.description:
European stocks fell in early trading on Tuesday, the dollar entered a second day of declines and U.S. Treasury yields hit a four-month high after U.S. President Donald Trump continued his push to take control of Greenland and threatened to reignite a trade war with Europe.

Trump said he no longer thought “purely of Peace” after he did not win the Nobel Peace Prize, and reiterated a threat to increase tariffs on EU members Denmark, Finland, France, Germany, Sweden, and the Netherlands, along with Britain and Norway, until the U.S. is allowed to buy Greenland.

EU leaders will discuss options, including tariffs worth 93 billion euros ($109 billion) on U.S. imports, at an emergency summit on Brussels on Thursday.

Stock markets fell, extending Monday’s declines, as the threats from the U.S. reignited a debate over the “Sell America” trade that emerged after Trump’s “Liberation Day” levies announced last April.E

Europe’s STOXX 600 was down 1.4 per cent on the day at 0956 GMT, having fallen 1.2 per cent on Monday, while the MSCI World Equity Index was down 0.2 per cent. The FTSE 100 was down 1.4 per cent.Trump separately threatened to hit French wines and champagne with 200 per cent tariffs, in an apparent effort to cajole French President Emmanuel Macron to join his Board of Peace initiative.

Amelie Derambure, senior multi-asset portfolio manager at Amundi in Paris, said that the downward move in markets was “precautionary profit-taking and some risk reduction” but that markets were helped by the macroeconomic backdrop. Recent economic data has pointed to growth and decelerating inflation, and her portfolios remain risk-orientated, she said.

“The situation in Greenland (is) worrying the markets. For the moment it remains relatively contained, there is no panic,” she said.

“I don’t see for the moment anything comparable to Liberation Day or to something very dramatic for markets,” she added.

The dollar index was down 0.6 per cent on the day, in its second day of declines, at 98.485, and the euro was up 0.7 per cent against the dollar at $$1.1726, its highest since January 6.As investors weighed up the risk of Trump reigniting a trade war with Europe over Greenland, U.S. Treasury yields hit their highest since September in early trading.

U.S. markets were closed on Monday for a public holiday, so the moves were a delayed reaction to the developments that began over the weekend. 

The spread between the U.S. 30-year yield and the 10-year yield, as well as the spread between the two-year and 10-year yield, were both set for their biggest one-day steepening since August 2025.

Elsewhere, Japanese government bond yields hit record highs over concerns that tax cuts will make the government’s finances worse. Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi had called a snap general election on Monday.

Oil prices edged higher, with Brent crude futures up 0.2 per cent at $64.01 a barrel and U.S. West Texas Intermediate up 0.5 per cent at $59.72 a barrel, as prices were supported by expectations for global economic growth. 

Reuters

data.title: Stock Picks: Unisem And Farm Fresh
data.description:
RHB Investment Bank Bhd (RHB Research) flagged Unisem (M) Bhd and Farm Fresh Bhd as technical stock picks, citing improving bullish momentum and potential upside from recent price action.

Unisem may extend its bullish trajectory after staging an upside breakout, RHB Research said.

The stock is trading above its 21-day simple moving average and has closed above the RM3.45 resistance level, signalling a bullish setup.

The research house said further upside could lift the counter towards the next resistance at RM3.60, followed by RM3.70.

However, a fall below the RM3.35 support level would negate the bullish view and signal a resumption of the correction phase.

Farm Fresh is also showing signs of further upside after testing its immediate resistance with strong trading volume.

RHB Research noted that the stock formed a long lower shadow in the previous session, indicating strengthening bullish momentum.

A decisive move above the RM2.85 resistance could see the counter advance towards RM3.00 and subsequently RM3.15.

Conversely, a decline below the RM2.70 support level could weaken sentiment and trigger a renewed correction.

data.title: Tariff Jitters Send Wall Street To Worst Day In Three Months
data.description:
US stocks suffered their steepest one-day losses in three months on Tuesday as investors reacted to renewed tariff threats from President Donald Trump, triggering a broad risk-off move across global markets.

All three major Wall Street indexes recorded their worst daily performance since October 10, with the S&P 500 and Nasdaq Composite both slipping below their 50-day moving averages.

The S&P 500 fell 143.15 points, or 2.06%, to close at 6,796.86, while the Nasdaq slid 561.07 points, or 2.39%, to 22,954.32.

The Dow Jones Industrial Average dropped 870.74 points, or 1.76%, to 48,488.59.

The selloff coincided with Trump’s weekend remarks, which marked the first opportunity for investors to respond following the Martin Luther King Jr. Day market holiday.

Trump said an additional 10% import tariff would take effect on February 1 on goods from Denmark, Norway, Sweden, France, Germany, the Netherlands, Finland and Great Britain, countries already subject to US tariffs.

He later said the tariffs would rise to 25% from June 1 and remain in place until an agreement was reached for the US to purchase Greenland, a proposal rejected by leaders in Greenland and Denmark, who insisted the island was not for sale.

The return of tariff rhetoric revived memories of April’s “Liberation Day”, when sweeping levies on global trading partners pushed the S&P 500 close to bear market territory.

Market volatility surged, with the CBOE Volatility Index closing at 20.09, its highest level since November 24.

Risk aversion rippled across asset classes. Gold surged to fresh record highs, US Treasury yields rose amid renewed selling pressure, and bitcoin fell more than 3%.

Trading volumes also climbed, with about 20.6 billion shares changing hands, well above the 20-day average of 17.01 billion.

Despite the sharp moves, some market participants questioned whether the reaction would have lasting consequences. Jamie Cox, managing partner at Harris Financial Group, said he did not see signs of a sustained investor exit.

“I’m not at the point yet where I’m willing to say what is happening with Greenland, and the resurgence of the tariff threat back ⁠and forth, is going to precipitate a ⁠correction in the equities markets,” he said, adding that he would be surprised to see a 3% to 5% market drop this week.

Bond markets also came under pressure globally. Japanese government bonds plunged, sending yields to record highs after Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi’s call for a snap election raised concerns over fiscal stability.

The selloff spilled into Europe, pushing up long-term bond yields, while losses in US Treasuries were most pronounced at the long end of the curve.

Looking ahead, investors are set to digest a slate of economic data this week, including third-quarter US GDP figures, January PMI readings and the Personal Consumption Expenditures report, the Federal Reserve’s preferred inflation gauge.

Corporate earnings season is also gaining momentum, with several major companies due to report, including Netflix, which closed 0.8% lower ahead of its earnings announcement.

Reuters

data.title: RHB Underweight On Rubber Products Sector
data.description:
RHB Investment Bank Bhd (RHB Research) maintained an UNDERWEIGHT rating on the rubber products sector, citing persistent earnings risks despite seemingly attractive valuations.

The research house highlighted that glove manufacturers are under pressure from a stronger RM and intensifying global competition, which may weigh on full-year 2026 earnings.

The brokerage’s latest outlook assumes global rubber glove consumption will stabilise in 2026, growing by 8% to roughly 400 billion pieces from 370 billion pieces in 2025, based on disclosures from Hartalega Bhd.

Growth is expected to moderate to around 6% in 2027, reaching 424 billion pieces, supported by structural improvements in global hygiene awareness and occupational safety standards.

However, analysts warned that further capacity expansions, particularly from China-linked producers, could add up to 80 billion pieces annually, potentially disrupting the demand-supply balance.

Average selling prices are expected to remain under pressure as manufacturers contend with incremental supply from China-based players and rising raw material costs.

While a weaker US dollar could lower RM-denominated input costs, it may simultaneously reduce pricing power, leaving producers vulnerable to margin compression in a price-sensitive market.

RHB Research noted that sector valuations appear inexpensive at 0.9 times forward price-to-book value, but this reflects ongoing structural headwinds.

Among the listed players, Top Glove Bhd is favoured for its focus on latex products, which analysts say provides a buffer against aggressive pricing in the nitrile segment dominated by China.

Weekly orders for the company have surged to 1.4 billion pieces, extending lead times beyond 60 days.

Despite this, RHB Research maintains a NEUTRAL rating on Top Glove, warning that rising latex costs could erode its competitive advantage.

The brokerage identified key risks that could alter the sector outlook, including fluctuations in the USD/RM rate, changes in glove selling prices, capacity expansion trends, raw material costs and tariff policies relative to competitors.

data.title: HLFG Leads Gainers As United Plantations Slips In Active Trading Session
data.description:
Bursa Malaysia saw rising movements today, with HLFG emerging as the top gainer while UTDPLT led the decliners.

HLFG (1082) rose RM0.56 to close at RM20.84, supported by active trading of 614,500 shares. Other notable gainers included DLADY (3026) up RM0.50 to RM31.78 on 38,400 shares, F&N (3689) gaining RM0.44 to RM36.16 with 327,900 shares traded, MPI (3867) advancing RM0.44 to RM34.00, and PETGAS (6033) adding RM0.38 to RM18.80 on 375,500 shares.

On the downside, UTDPLT (2089) fell RM0.76 to RM30.30 with 424,600 shares exchanged. Nestle (4707) dipped RM0.40 to RM115.60 on 25,600 shares, BLDPLNT (5069) lost RM0.30 to RM15.30, FAREAST (5029) declined RM0.25 to RM3.95, and KLK (2445) slipped RM0.20 to RM20.00 with 542,900 shares traded.

The session reflected cautious investor sentiment, with gains concentrated in finance and consumer sectors, while selective selling weighed on plantation counters.

data.title: Bursa Ends Higher As FBM KLCI Gains 6.75 Points
data.description:
Bursa Malaysia closed on a positive note today, with the benchmark FBMKLCI rising 6.75 points or 0.40% to 1,705.81, supported by broad-based buying in both large-cap and mid-cap stocks.

The FBM70 index added 54.78 points, or 0.31%, to 17,452.61, while the FBMEMAS and FBMSHA indices climbed 49.74 points (+0.40%) and 50.78 points (+0.42%) to close at 12,564.94 and 12,243.49 respectively.

The F4GBM index gained 4.20 points (+0.42%) to 1,011.58.

On the local bourse, trading activity remained robust with Velesto (5243) leading the most active counters, closing at RM0.29 with nearly 99.9 million shares exchanged.

Zetrix (0138) followed at RM0.815 with 95.9 million shares traded, while PMETAL (8869) jumped 16 sen to RM7.40 on 70.6 million shares.

Other active stocks included SBS (0386) and Tanco (2429), closing at RM0.23 and RM1.29 respectively.

Market sentiment was lifted by gains in the finance, construction and metal sectors, offsetting selective selling in smaller counters.

Investors appeared cautious ahead of upcoming corporate earnings announcements and regional market developments.

data.title: Bursa Opens In The Red As FBM KLCI Dips 0.21%
data.description:
Bursa Malaysia opened lower, with the benchmark FBM KLCI (FBMKLCI) shedding 3.61 points, or 0.21%, to 1,695.45 at 9.06 am, as cautious trading dominated early market sentiment.

Other key indices also trended down. The FBM70 slipped 14.02 points (-0.08%) to 17,383.81, the F4GBM lost 1.81 points (-0.18%) to 1,005.57, while broader market measures FBMEMAS and FBMSHA fell 25.04 points (-0.20%) and 28.68 points (-0.24%) respectively.

In the early session, trading was led by heavy turnover in select counters. SBS ([S] 0386) emerged as the most active stock with 5.43 million shares traded, rising 1 sen to 25 sen.

It was followed by VS ([S] 6963) at 46 sen, with 4.51 million shares, and Zetrix ([S] 0138) at 80.5 sen, with 22.26 million units moving.

Among the top gainers, HEIM ([S] 3255) led with a 16 sen increase to RM22.66, while F&N ([S] 3689) rose 8 sen to RM35.80. On the flip side, KLK ([S] 2445) and MPI ([S] 3867) were among the top decliners, falling 16 sen each to RM20.04 and RM33.40 respectively.

Early activity suggests a cautious market as investors await developments in domestic and global economies, with selective buying observed in consumer and small-cap stocks.

data.title: Bursa Opens Higher, FBM KLCI Up 0.42%
data.description:
Bursa Malaysia opened higher on Thursday, with the benchmark FTSE Bursa Malaysia KLCI (FBMKLCI) rising 7.09 points, or 0.42%, to 1,712.90 at 9.10 am.

The broader market also showed gains, with the FBM70 up 87.58 points (+0.50%) to 17,540.19, the F4GBM climbing 4.36 points (+0.43%) to 1,015.94 and the FBMEMAS advancing 52.01 points (+0.41%) to 12,616.95.

The FBMSHA rose 46.74 points (+0.38%) to 12,290.23.

On the active board, SMRT led trading with 35.24 million shares at RM0.210, up 2 sen.

Debutant Guan Huat Seng Holdings (GHS), which recently made its ACE Market debut, was actively traded at RM0.240, down 1 sen, with 12.22 million shares changing hands.

Other active counters included Velesto at RM0.290 and IJM at RM2.570.

Top gainers in early trading were Nestle (+0.60 sen), Allianz (+0.36 sen), and United Plantations (+0.30 sen), while Mercury (-0.055 sen), Zhulian (-0.05 sen) and Hextech (-0.04 sen) led the losers.

Market sentiment at the opening appeared cautious but generally positive, as investors assessed early trading movements.

data.title: SGX Opens Higher, STI Gains 22.97 Points On Broad-Based Buying
data.description:
The Singapore Exchange (SGX) opened higher on Thursday, with the Straits Times Index (STI) rising 22.97 points, or 0.48%, to 4,832.85 as of 9.09 am.

Market sentiment was broadly positive, with advancers outnumbering decliners 158 to 52 on the day, and a total trading volume of 314.25 million shares worth S$192.07 million.

Key gainers included UOB, which rose 1.52% to S$37.33, and OCBC Bank, up modestly to S$20.51.

DBS and Singtel were also in positive territory, reflecting early confidence in financial and telecom sectors. Among small caps, MarcoPolo Marine traded at S$0.163, while YZJ Shipbuilding was at S$3.46.

Other indices tracking sectoral performance were mixed. The iEdge Singapore Next 50 Index stood at 1,483.97, while the iEdge S-REIT Index was at 1,126.60.

Regional and thematic indices such as the iEdge-OCBC Singapore Low Carbon Select 40 Capped Index and iEdge Southeast Asia+ TECH Index also saw modest gains.

Derivatives trading showed 36,000 contracts exchanged, with futures contracts including MSCI Singapore Index Futures at 452.95 and FTSE China A50 Index Futures at 15,031.00.

Early moves in currency futures reflected USD strength, with USD/SGD futures at 1.2808 and USD/CNH at 6.9372.

The market opened cautiously optimistic, tracking gains in regional peers as investors digest global economic developments and earnings updates.

data.title: Trump’s Greenland Tariff Threats Rattle Markets, Spur ‘Sell America’ Trades
data.description:
Asian stocks fell on Tuesday while the dollar remained under pressure and US Treasury yields climbed to their highest level in more than four months, as renewed trade-war concerns rattled risk sentiment.

Citi has downgraded European equities, while analysts said gold remained near record highs as investors sought safe-haven assets amid uncertainty over US-Europe trade relations.

The market turbulence follows US President Donald Trump’s threats to impose additional tariffs over Greenland, a move that risks intensifying trade tensions with Europe.

Futures for the Nasdaq and S&P 500 fell around 1% in early trade, while the yield on the 10-year US Treasury note rose to 4.265%, marking its highest level since early September.

The MSCI Asia-Pacific index outside Japan declined 0.44%, pulling further from last week’s record highs.

MUFG’s Europe economist Henry Cook said last year had shown not to overreact to Trump’s threats, but added that the latest remarks could keep tariff uncertainty elevated, even if de-escalation occurs.

Citi strategists noted that the heightened tensions weaken the near-term investment case for European equities and cast doubt on a broad-based earnings inflection in 2026. European futures opened 0.12% lower.

Investors are now eyeing the World Economic Forum in Davos, where Trump is expected to meet global business leaders on Wednesday.

Meanwhile, Japan’s Nikkei dropped 0.8% as the market anticipates the upcoming election, with Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi campaigning for tax cuts, higher spending, and a new security strategy.

Long- and short-term Japanese government bond yields surged on Monday amid concerns that tax cuts could worsen the country’s fiscal position.

Gold remained largely unchanged at US$4,670 per ounce, slightly below Monday’s record high, reflecting continued safe-haven demand.

The episode revives discussions of a “Sell America” trade, where investors offload US equities, the dollar, and Treasuries in response to policy uncertainties.

Analysts caution that any demand spike is likely short-lived, and structural supply pressures in key markets remain the main long-term concern.

Reuters

data.title: STI Loses 0.7% As Traders Stay On Sidelines
data.description:
The Singapore Exchange (SGX) opened lower on Wednesday, with the Straits Times Index (STI) dropping 34.45 points, or 0.71%, to 4,793.55 amid cautious trading.

Early market activity saw 55 advancers and 142 decliners, with total volume reaching 98.08 million shares valued at S$145.85 million.

Among top movers, UOB traded at S$36.37, OCBC Bank at S$20.31, Singtel at S$4.55, YZJ Shipbuilding at S$3.51, CapitaLand Investment at S$2.93, and ST Engineering at S$9.53.

The iEdge Singapore Next 50 Index opened at 1,484.99, while the iEdge S-REIT Index stood at 1,133.02. The iEdge-OCBC Singapore Low Carbon Select 40 Capped Index was at 3,684.00, and the iEdge APAC Financials Dividend Plus Index opened at 3,584.63.

SGX derivatives also saw activity, with 12,530 contracts traded, including the Nikkei 225 Index Futures at 52,640.00 and the FTSE China A50 Index Futures at 15,067.00.

Traders remained cautious ahead of regional economic updates and global market developments, keeping early sentiment subdued.

data.title: Bursa Stumbles Past 1,700 As SBS Leads Activity, Capital A Shines
data.description:
Bursa Malaysia edged lower at midday, with the benchmark FBM KLCI slipping 9.17 points or 0.54% to 1,703.16 as of 12.30 pm.

The broader market mirrored the retreat, with the FBM70 down 95.73 points (-0.55%) to 17,427.03 and the FBM Emas Index easing 64.90 points (-0.51%) to 12,546.64.

Trading activity was led by debutant SBS Bhd, which fell 1 sen to RM0.24 on volume of 66.1 million shares, followed by ZETRIX Bhd at RM0.805 with 45.3 million shares traded.

Capital A Bhd bucked the trend, rising 3 sen to RM0.535 on 39.7 million shares, while IJM Corp slipped 11 sen to RM2.54 with 38.9 million shares changing hands.

Among gainers, TimeCom Resources Bhd led the rally with a 15 sen gain to RM5.70, followed by Infomedia Asia Bhd (+12 sen to RM1.54), KLCC Bhd (+10 sen to RM9.11), LPI Capital Bhd (+10 sen to RM15.30) and Westports Holdings Bhd (+9 sen to RM6.00).

On the downside, Hong Leong Bank Bhd fell 66 sen to RM23.64, while Hong Leong Financial Group Bhd declined 66 sen to RM20.92.

Heineken Malaysia Bhd eased 50 sen to RM22.50, Nestlé (Malaysia) Bhd lost 30 sen to RM115.70 and UTD Plantations Bhd dropped 30 sen to RM30.90.

Market watchers noted the mixed momentum as investors weighed corporate earnings and ongoing global trade concerns, with selective buying in counters such as Capital A and LPI Capital supporting the market’s resilience.

data.title: Ringgit Opens Firmer On Modest Demand Amid Cautious Trading
data.description:
The ringgit opened slightly stronger against the US dollar and other major currencies on Wednesday, supported by modest demand as traders remained cautious amid ongoing economic developments.

At 8 am, the local currency traded at 4.0525/0575 against the greenback, compared with Tuesday’s close of 4.0530/0580.

The ringgit also gained against a basket of major currencies. It rose against the Japanese yen to 2.5615/5648 from 2.5696/5729, edged up versus the British pound to 5.4462/4529 from 5.4618/4686, and appreciated against the euro to 4.7516/7574 from 4.7530/7588.

Against regional currencies, the local note was mostly firmer. It strengthened versus the Singapore dollar to 3.1557/1598 from 3.1602/1646 and against the Indonesian rupiah to 238.7/239.4 from 239.0/239.4.

However, the ringgit slipped slightly against the Thai baht to 13.0566/0824 from 13.0397/0629 and remained unchanged versus the Philippine peso at 6.81/6.83.
